name: scarce sugar cane
shortDescription: Scarce sugar cane cut in firm, pale-green lengths, sweet enough to make bakers argue over the bundle before it reaches the stall. It travels badly in heat and sells quickly in towns where syrup, candy, and festival cakes are more profitable than plain grain.
flavorText: “A stalk of sugar is a promise that children understand before merchants do.”

name: apple
shortDescription: A crisp apple with bright skin, firm flesh, and the honest market appeal of fruit that can survive a cart ride. Common enough for lunch, useful enough for baking, and still welcome in any pack that has seen too much hard bread.
flavorText: “An apple is small proof that the road has not beaten all sweetness from the world.”

name: avocado
shortDescription: A dark green avocado with soft rich flesh hidden under a tough skin. Traders prize it for coastal tables, healer diets, and fashionable cooks who insist that plain bread becomes noble once something creamy is spread across it.
flavorText: “The fruit looks armored because it knows how quickly softness is stolen.”

name: banana
shortDescription: A curved yellow banana from warmer roads, sweet, soft, and easy to sell before bruises tell the whole story. Porters like them for quick strength, children like them for obvious reasons, and innkeepers like anything that needs no knife.
flavorText: “A banana ripens faster than a bargain, which is why both must be handled early.”

name: bartlett pears
shortDescription: Bartlett pears with smooth skins and fragrant flesh, delicate enough to punish careless packing. Their sweetness deepens near ripeness, making them a favorite of dessert cooks and a gamble for merchants who cannot control the weather.
flavorText: “Pears teach patience, then punish hesitation.”

name: breadfruit
shortDescription: A heavy breadfruit with rough green skin and starchy flesh that fills a belly like honest baking. In hungry ports it is treated less like fruit and more like a quiet miracle, good for roasting, boiling, and stretching thin stores.
flavorText: “Some trees grow fruit. Kinder trees grow supper.”

name: coconut
shortDescription: A hard-shelled coconut carrying sweet water and rich white flesh behind a stubborn husk. Sailors, cooks, and oil pressers all find a use for it, though everyone agrees the first challenge is convincing it to open.
flavorText: “The sea sends gifts wrapped like locked chests.”

name: dragon fruit
shortDescription: A vivid dragon fruit with scaled skin, bright flesh, and a name that does half the selling before anyone tastes it. Nobles buy it for color, alchemists buy it for rumors, and fruit sellers buy it because crowds gather around strange things.
flavorText: “Not every dragon breathes fire. Some arrive in baskets and stain the knife pink.”

name: figs
shortDescription: Soft figs with tender skins and a honeyed center, prized fresh when roads are short and dried when caravans are long. They suit breakfast boards, temple offerings, and the private pockets of guards who claim not to like sweets.
flavorText: “A fig is modest outside and lavish within, like a banker pretending to be poor.”

name: jackfruit
shortDescription: A massive jackfruit with a spiked rind, sticky sap, and a harvest large enough to change a cook's whole plan. Its flesh can feed crowds, fill pies, or stand in for richer fare when coin is short and spice is clever.
flavorText: “One jackfruit enters the kitchen and suddenly everyone has an opinion.”

name: kiwi
shortDescription: A small fuzzy kiwi with sharp green flesh and bright tart sweetness. It looks unimpressive in a crate until sliced, at which point cooks, children, and fruit sellers all remember that surprise is part of value.
flavorText: “Never judge a fruit until the knife has had its say.”

name: lemon
shortDescription: A yellow lemon with sour juice, clean fragrance, and more uses than its size suggests. It sharpens fish, wakes sauces, cleans copper, and gives traveling cooks a way to make stale food seem accused rather than spoiled.
flavorText: “A lemon does not flatter a meal. It tells the truth loudly.”

name: lime
shortDescription: A small green lime packed with bright acid and a smell that cuts through smoke, salt, and market dust. Sailors trust it, cooks squeeze it over everything, and drink sellers know it can make cheap spirits seem almost intentional.
flavorText: “Lime is the spark that teaches dull food to answer back.”

name: mango
shortDescription: A ripe mango with golden flesh, fragrant juice, and a softness that demands careful handling. It sells best when the sun is high and customers are thirsty, because no argument for sweetness is stronger than the smell of one opened fruit.
flavorText: “A mango does not travel quietly; its perfume announces the whole cargo.”

name: melons
shortDescription: Round melons with thick rinds and cool sweet flesh, bulky in a wagon but beloved at summer markets. A good melon can refresh laborers, decorate feast tables, and turn a dusty stall into the most visited corner of the square.
flavorText: “On hot days, a sliced melon is worth more than a sermon.”

name: papaya
shortDescription: A soft orange papaya with mellow sweetness and a tropical scent that lingers around the crate. Cooks use it fresh, healers praise it after heavy meals, and traders sell it quickly before ripeness becomes complaint.
flavorText: “Papaya is sunshine pretending to be medicine.”

name: peach
shortDescription: A blushing peach with soft skin, fragrant flesh, and the terrible habit of bruising when handled by fools. It is worth the care, especially for pies, preserves, and noble breakfasts served beside gossip and porcelain.
flavorText: “A peach forgives neither rough hands nor late buyers.”

name: pineapple
shortDescription: A crowned pineapple with armored skin, sweet yellow flesh, and enough spectacle to make it a centerpiece before it becomes food. Inns display it to look prosperous, captains gift it to look worldly, and cooks fight the rind for the prize inside.
flavorText: “Few fruits arrive already wearing a crown.”

name: plums
shortDescription: Deep-colored plums with smooth skins and juicy flesh, sweet near the stone and tart enough to keep the mouth interested. They are good fresh, better stewed, and profitable dried when caravans need fruit that can endure bad roads.
flavorText: “A plum carries summer in a purple coat.”

name: pomegranate
shortDescription: A red pomegranate packed with jewel-like seeds and staining juice, prized as much for appearance as flavor. Priests call it symbolic, cooks call it useful, and merchants call it expensive when opened carefully in front of customers.
flavorText: “Some fruits hide coins. This one hides rubies that can be eaten.”

name: pumpkins
shortDescription: Sturdy pumpkins with thick orange skins and sweet earthy flesh, dependable in cellars and generous in kitchens. They fill soups, pies, stews, and winter bellies, which makes them less glamorous than rare fruit but far more loyal.
flavorText: “A pumpkin is a lantern, a meal, and a storage plan wearing one skin.”

name: squash
shortDescription: A firm squash with dense flesh and a hard rind built for storage, trade, and rough carts. It takes well to roasting, herbs, and patience, giving poor kitchens bulk and rich kitchens something humble to pretend they discovered.
flavorText: “Squash survives the road because it expects little kindness from it.”

name: starfruit
shortDescription: A yellow starfruit with ridged sides that cut into bright star-shaped slices. Its flavor is light, crisp, and slightly tart, but its real strength is making plates look festive without requiring a court artist.
flavorText: “The sky loses stars, and fruit sellers find them.”

name: strawberry
shortDescription: A red strawberry with tiny seeds, soft flesh, and a sweetness too fragile for careless trade. It sells best near gardens and festivals, where short travel keeps it pretty and customers are already willing to forgive luxury.
flavorText: “Strawberries are proof that some profits must be made quickly.”

name: tomatoes
shortDescription: Ripe tomatoes with glossy skins and bright red flesh, useful raw, stewed, roasted, or turned into sauces that stain every honest apron. Their value depends on freshness, firmness, and how loudly local cooks argue about them.
flavorText: “A tomato is a fruit until it enters a kitchen, then it becomes politics.”

name: grapes
shortDescription: Clusters of grapes with taut skins, sweet juice, and the pleasant danger of becoming wine if nobody eats them first. They travel in shallow baskets, attract wasps, and make both vintners and children reach faster than manners allow.
flavorText: “Every grape is either a snack or the beginning of a bad decision.”

name: backribs
shortDescription: A rack of backribs with good fat, clean bone, and the promise of smoke, spice, and patient cooking. Butchers move them quickly near feast days, while taverns know that ribs make customers forget how much ale they ordered.
flavorText: “Ribs are eaten with fingers because forks have too much dignity.”

name: bacon
shortDescription: Strips of salted bacon, rich with fat and smoke, useful in poor pots and noble breakfasts alike. It keeps better than fresh pork, perfumes whatever it touches, and has ended more arguments at campfires than diplomacy ever managed.
flavorText: “Bacon is what happens when preservation learns showmanship.”

name: chicken leg
shortDescription: A chicken leg with crisping skin and enough meat for a quick meal, a tavern plate, or a traveler's impatient appetite. It is simple food, but simple food sells well when the smell of roasting fat reaches the street.
flavorText: “No one asks a chicken leg for pedigree.”

name: chop
shortDescription: A clean meat chop cut thick enough for pan, grill, or noble plate, with fat at the edge where flavor waits. It is honest butcher's work: recognizable, quick to cook, and easy to price by eye and hunger.
flavorText: “A chop is meat made polite enough for a plate.”

name: cooked chicken
shortDescription: Cooked chicken with browned skin, tender meat, and the universal appeal of food that needs no explanation. It suits tavern tables, road baskets, and sickrooms, proving that plain fare becomes valuable when it is hot and ready.
flavorText: “A roasted bird turns strangers into table companions faster than introductions.”

name: cooked hog
shortDescription: A cooked hog prepared for a crowd, rich with roasted skin, heavy meat, and the smell of serious celebration. It is not bought for solitude; it belongs to fairs, weddings, mercenary bonuses, and nights when restraint has been dismissed.
flavorText: “A whole hog is a feast that refuses to whisper.”

name: ham
shortDescription: A cured ham with deep flavor, firm meat, and enough salt to survive long roads and slow winters. Sliced thin it serves nobles, cut thick it feeds workers, and hung from a beam it makes a pantry look confident.
flavorText: “Ham is patience, salt, and a pig remembered fondly.”

name: liver
shortDescription: Fresh liver, dark, rich, and prized by cooks who know how quickly strength can turn bitter in the pan. Healers praise it, apprentices fear it, and good kitchens hide its edge under onions, herbs, and confidence.
flavorText: “Liver rewards skill and punishes hesitation.”

name: meatballs
shortDescription: Seasoned meatballs rolled for stew pots, skewers, or sauce, small enough to count and tempting enough to disappear before counting is finished. Taverns like them because scraps become comfort when minced, spiced, and browned properly.
flavorText: “A meatball is a butcher's apology made delicious.”

name: pig kebab
shortDescription: A pig kebab threaded with chunks of pork ready for fire, smoke, and street-corner hunger. It is practical food for markets: easy to turn, easy to smell from far away, and impossible to eat while pretending not to enjoy it.
flavorText: “Put meat on a stick and civilization becomes portable.”

name: porkchop
shortDescription: A porkchop with pale meat, a neat bone, and a strip of fat that promises flavor once heat finds it. It is a butcher's reliable sale, equally comfortable in farmhouse skillets and polished inns that rename simple things.
flavorText: “The bone is there to remind the diner this was once less negotiable.”

name: rib roast
shortDescription: A rib roast cut for ceremony, thick with meat and framed by bones that make any table look wealthy. It asks for slow heat, sharp knives, and enough guests to justify the size, though leftovers rarely offend anyone.
flavorText: “A rib roast is how a kitchen announces that caution has left the house.”

name: sausage
shortDescription: A coil of sausage packed with seasoned meat, fat, and the butcher's private opinion of spice. Durable, flexible, and profitable, it can rescue thin soup, fill bread, or feed guards who refuse to discuss ingredients.
flavorText: “Sausage proves that mystery sells better when browned.”

name: steak
shortDescription: A thick steak cut for hot iron and impatient appetites, valued by marbling, color, and the promise of a clean sear. It is direct food for direct buyers, especially those who believe supper should arrive with a knife.
flavorText: “A steak is not subtle, and neither are its best customers.”

name: tbone
shortDescription: A T-bone steak with its proud center bone separating two cuts of rich meat. It looks impressive before cooking and better after, making it a favorite for taverns that want one plate to silence a hungry room.
flavorText: “The T stands for triumph, according to butchers with good salesmanship.”

name: cooked turkey
shortDescription: Cooked turkey with broad browned skin, plentiful meat, and the ceremonial weight of a meal meant for many. It suits harvest tables, guild banquets, and any household trying to prove that lean times have politely stepped outside.
flavorText: “A turkey is a bird that arrives already planning leftovers.”

name: turkey leg
shortDescription: A heavy turkey leg, roasted or ready for roasting, built for fairs, campfires, and diners who prefer their meals to have handles. It is large, savory, and dramatic enough to make even quiet eaters look victorious.
flavorText: “Some meals are held like trophies because that is what they are.”

name: black morels
shortDescription: Black morels with honeycombed caps, earthy perfume, and the kind of value that makes foragers suddenly vague about where they were found. They demand careful cleaning and reward cooks with a flavor deeper than their weight suggests.
flavorText: “The best mushroom patches are described only as ‘north of here’.”

name: cremini mushrooms
shortDescription: Cremini mushrooms with brown caps, firm flesh, and a dependable savory flavor for stews, pies, and pan sauces. They are not flashy, which is why serious cooks trust them; not every good ingredient needs a rumor attached.
flavorText: “Cremini mushrooms are humble until the butter touches them.”

name: enoki mushroom
shortDescription: Enoki mushrooms with long pale stems and tiny caps, delicate as thread but lively in soups and careful plates. They bruise easily, sell best fresh, and make simple broth look like someone planned it with refinement.
flavorText: “Enoki are mushrooms drawn with a fine brush.”

name: scarce king trumpet mushrooms
shortDescription: Scarce king trumpet mushrooms with thick white stems, small caps, and a meaty bite prized by cooks who know their value. They roast beautifully, slice cleanly, and command respect from buyers who normally ignore vegetables.
flavorText: “A king trumpet does not need a crown; it has the stem for it.”

name: portobello mushrooms
shortDescription: Portobello mushrooms with broad dark caps and a hearty flavor that can stand beside meat without apologizing. Grillers, pie makers, and tavern cooks buy them when they need something filling, earthy, and large enough to impress.
flavorText: “Portobellos are what mushrooms become when they stop being polite.”

name: almond
shortDescription: An almond with a hard shell, pale nut, and a clean sweetness that suits pastry, oil, and long storage. It is small, but merchants like small goods that keep their value and do not complain about being packed tightly.
flavorText: “An almond is a locked bite of orchard patience.”

name: peanut
shortDescription: A peanut in its rough shell, common, filling, and easy to roast by the handful. It is the sort of humble trade good that feeds workers, fills bowls in taverns, and earns steady coin without ever looking expensive.
flavorText: “Peanuts are proof that a small profit can still make a loud crunch.”
